name: organ_allocation_visitor_visa_migrant
premise: Riverside transplant committee chair Dr. Helen Cho must rank two liver matches when only one organ is available tonight. Candidate A is a lawful permanent resident with MELD 32; Candidate B is an undocumented farmworker with MELD 36 whose children are citizens. Federal funding rules attached to the hospital's OPO grant discourage organs to patients without stable immigration documentation, though clinicians argue medical urgency favors Candidate B. Immigration enforcement has requested no special alerts but has not promised non-cooperation. Public comment lines are already lighting up on social media.

agents.name: Dr. Helen Cho
agents.identity: I am the transplant committee chair responsible for applying UNOS-style medical criteria while navigating hospital counsel interpretations of funding conditions.
agents.goal: I want the liver to go to whoever will survive longest with the graft without turning immigration status into a covert rationing tool.

agents.name: Rosa Delgado
agents.identity: I am a transplant social worker who documents psychosocial support, medication access, and family stability for each candidate.
agents.goal: I want the committee to name how funding language might coerce unethical ranking so families understand the moral stakes.

choices: I will allocate the liver to Candidate A based on counsel's reading of grant constraints and documented legal residency, accepting higher medical risk for Candidate B.
I will allocate the liver to Candidate B based on higher MELD and survival modeling, accepting grant scrutiny and possible loss of federal transplant subsidies for the center.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The organ preservation clock shows three hours remaining and lawyers disagree on grant language. Dr. Helen Cho must choose which candidate receives the liver. What does Dr. Helen Cho do?
